passages.explanation: Jesus goes to his hometown, and his disciples follow. On the Sabbath he teaches in the synagogue. Many who hear him are amazed: where did this man get these things? What's this wisdom? What are these remarkable miracles? Isn't this the carpenter? Isn't this Mary's son and the brother of James, Joseph, Judas, and Simon? Aren't his sisters here with us? They take offence at him. Jesus says: a prophet is not without honour except in his own town, among his relatives, and in his own home. He can't do any miracles there, except lay his hands on a few sick people and heal them. He's amazed at their lack of faith. Nazareth can't get past his ordinariness. They know his family, his trade, his childhood — and that familiarity blocks their faith. Mark says he 'could not' do miracles there, not 'would not.' Faith creates the environment where God's power operates.

Jesus calls the Twelve and begins to send them out two by two, giving them authority over impure spirits. He instructs them to take nothing for the journey except a staff — no bread, no bag, no money in their belts. Wear sandals but not an extra shirt. Whenever you enter a house, stay there until you leave that town. And if any place won't welcome you or listen to you, leave that place and shake the dust off your feet as a testimony against them. They go out, preach that people should repent, drive out many demons, and anoint many sick people with oil and heal them. The mission is deliberately under-resourced — the disciples must depend on God and on the hospitality of those they serve. They go in pairs for mutual support and credibility. The authority Jesus has, he delegates.

King Herod hears about Jesus — his name has become well known. Some say John the Baptist has been raised from the dead. Others say it's Elijah. Still others say he's a prophet. But when Herod hears this, he says: John, whom I beheaded — he has been raised! Mark then flashes back to explain: Herod had arrested John, bound him, and put him in prison because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife, whom Herod had married. John had been telling Herod: it's not lawful for you to have your brother's wife. Herodias nurses a grudge against John and wants to kill him, but Herod fears John, knowing him to be a righteous and holy man, and protects him. When Herod hears John, he's greatly puzzled — yet he likes to listen to him. The opportune time comes when Herod holds a banquet for his high officials, military commanders, and the leading men of Galilee. Herodias' daughter dances and pleases Herod and his dinner guests. The king says: ask me for anything and I'll give it to you — up to half my kingdom. She goes to her mother: what shall I ask for? The head of John the Baptist. She hurries back: I want you to give me right now the head of John the Baptist on a platter. The king is greatly distressed, but because of his oaths and his dinner guests, he doesn't want to refuse. He sends an executioner, who beheads John in prison, brings the head on a platter, and presents it to the girl, who gives it to her mother. John's disciples come, take the body, and lay it in a tomb. Mark places this story between the sending and return of the Twelve — while the disciples are out preaching, the forerunner is killed. The cost of faithfulness is established.

The apostles gather around Jesus and report all they've done and taught. He says: come with me by yourselves to a quiet place and get some rest. So many people are coming and going that they don't even have a chance to eat. They go away by themselves in a boat to a solitary place. But many who see them leaving recognise them and run on foot from all the towns, arriving ahead of them. When Jesus lands and sees a large crowd, he has compassion on them because they're like sheep without a shepherd. He begins teaching them many things. By late afternoon the disciples come to him: this is a remote place, and it's already very late. Send the people away so they can buy themselves something to eat. Jesus answers: you give them something to eat. They say: that would take more than half a year's wages! Are we to go and spend that much on bread? He asks: how many loaves do you have? Go and see. Five, they say, and two fish. He directs them to have all the people sit down in groups on the green grass — in groups of hundreds and fifties. He takes the five loaves and two fish, looks up to heaven, gives thanks, breaks the loaves, and gives them to the disciples to distribute. He also divides the two fish among them all. They all eat and are satisfied. The disciples pick up twelve baskets of broken pieces of bread and fish. The number of men who ate is five thousand. Jesus wanted rest. The crowd prevented it. He responded with compassion, not irritation. The feeding echoes God providing manna in the wilderness — the shepherd feeds his sheep. Twelve baskets remain: one for each apostle who doubted there was enough.

Immediately Jesus makes his disciples get into the boat and go ahead to Bethsaida, while he dismisses the crowd. After leaving them, he goes up on a mountainside to pray. Later that night the boat is in the middle of the lake, and he's alone on land. He sees the disciples straining at the oars because the wind is against them. Shortly before dawn he goes out to them, walking on the lake. He's about to pass by them, but when they see him walking on the lake, they think he's a ghost and cry out — they all see him and are terrified. Immediately he speaks: take courage! It's me. Don't be afraid. He climbs into the boat and the wind dies down. They're completely amazed, because they hadn't understood about the loaves — their hearts were hardened. The phrase 'about to pass by them' echoes God 'passing by' Moses in Exodus 33 and Elijah in 1 Kings 19 — divine self-revelation. Mark connects the disciples' failure to understand the walking on water to their failure to understand the feeding. If they'd grasped who fed the five thousand, they wouldn't be surprised to see him walking on water.

When they cross over, they land at Gennesaret and anchor there. As soon as they get out of the boat, people recognise Jesus. They run throughout the whole region, carrying the sick on mats to wherever they hear he is. Wherever he goes — villages, towns, or countryside — they place the sick in the marketplaces and beg him to let them touch even the edge of his cloak. And all who touch it are healed. The demand is relentless. Jesus can't go anywhere without being swarmed. The faith of the crowds mirrors the woman with the bleeding — they believe that even his clothing carries healing power.
